case_name: Lowenhaupt v. North Shore Univ. Hosp. (Northwell Health)

summary.brief: Lowenhaupt v. North Shore Univ. Hosp. (Northwell Health). Medical malpractice case involving emergency medicine.

evidence_index.text: The plaintiff allegedly was injured as a result of negligence and medical malpractice while receiving treatment in the defendant's emergency department in December 2019 when a member of the defendant's staff discarded a severed portion of the plaintiff's finger.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: ORDERED that the judgment is reversed, on the law and in the exercise of discretion, that branch of the defendant's motion which was pursuant to CPLR 3012(b) to dismiss the complaint for failure to timely serve the complaint is denied, the plaintiff's motion pursuant to CPLR 2004 and, in effect, 3012(d) to extend the time to serve the complaint upon the defendant and to compel the defendant to accept late service of the complaint is granted, the complaint is reinstated, and the orders are modified accordingly.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The plaintiff also demonstrated that he had a potentially meritorious cause of action by submitting his verification of the complaint, his affidavit detailing the incident, and, to the extent that the action sounded in medical malpractice, an affirmation of an expert physician who opined that, based on the existing record, the defendant had deviated from the accepted standard of care with regard to preservation and evaluation of traumatically severed body parts in a hospital emergency department, resulting in injuries to the plaintiff.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Contrary to the Supreme Court's determination, the plaintiff demonstrated a reasonable excuse for the delay in serving the complaint. Law office failure can be a reasonable justification for untimely service of a complaint after a demand for complaint has been served.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented to the emergency department with a traumatically severed portion of a finger.

focused_exam.system: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

requestables.name: Surgical consultation
requestables.description: Consultation for management of severed finger

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with severed finger

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with severed finger
decision_points.prompt: What should be done with the severed finger?

options.description: Preserve the severed finger for potential reattachment
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care involves preservation for possible reattachment

expected_action_court.description: Severed finger should have been preserved
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established preservation is standard

explanation.what_should_have_happened: Severed finger should have been preserved for potential reattachment

patient_outcome.description: Loss of finger due to inability to reattach
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Loss of function in affected hand

key_findings: Breach of duty in discarding severed finger
Causation established - preservation could have allowed reattachment
